full_response:
We start from the defining relation between the metric and its inverse  

g^{\nu\rho}\,g_{\rho\sigma}= \delta^{\nu}{}_{\sigma}.     (1)

The Kronecker delta is just a set of constants, so its ordinary derivative vanishes:

∂_μ( g^{\nu\rho} g_{\rho\sigma} ) = 0.     (2)

Apply the product rule to (2):

(∂_μ g^{\nu\rho})\,g_{\rho\sigma} + g^{\nu\rho}\,∂_μ g_{\rho\sigma} = 0.     (3)

To isolate ∂_μ g^{\nu\rho}, raise the index σ in (3) by multiplying with g^{\sigma\lambda}:

(∂_μ g^{\nu\rho})\,g_{\rho\sigma}\,g^{\sigma\lambda}
      + g^{\nu\rho}\,∂_μ g_{\rho\sigma}\,g^{\sigma\lambda} = 0.     (4)

Because g_{\rho\sigma} g^{\sigma\lambda}=δ_{\rho}^{\;\lambda}, the first term simplifies:

(∂_μ g^{\nu\rho})\,δ_{\rho}^{\;\lambda}=∂_μ g^{\nu\lambda}.     (5)

Renaming the dummy index λ→ρ and the pair (ρ,σ)→(κ,λ) in the second term gives

∂_μ g^{\nu\rho}= -\,g^{\nuκ}\,g^{ρλ}\,∂_μ g_{λκ}.     (6)

Since g_{λκ}=g_{κλ} (the metric is symmetric), expression (6) is the desired result.

\boxed{\,\partial_\mu g^{\nu\rho} = -\, g^{\nu\kappa}\, g^{\rho\lambda}\,\partial_\mu g_{\lambda\kappa}\,}

Confidence: 93%
